name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ ۖ فَإِن تَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا (٥٩)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you. And if you dispute concerning anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle, if you have faith in Allah and the Last Day. That is better and more favourable in outcome. (59)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَزْعُمُونَ أَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَقَدْ أُمِرُوٓا۟ أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَيُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَن يُضِلَّهُمْ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (٦٠)
```

```english
Have you not regarded those who claim that they believe in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you? They desire to seek the judgment of fake deities, though they were commanded to reject them, and Satan desires to lead them astray into far error. (60)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ رَأَيْتَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنكَ صُدُودًا (٦١)
```

```english
When they are told, ‘Come to what Allah has sent down and [come] to the Apostle,’ you see the hypocrites keep away from you aversely. (61)
```

```arabic
فَكَيْفَ إِذَآ أَصَـٰبَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ جَآءُوكَ يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَآ إِلَّآ إِحْسَـٰنًا وَتَوْفِيقًا (٦٢)
```

```english
But how will it be when an affliction visits them because of what their hands have sent ahead? Then they will come to you, swearing by Allah, ‘We desired nothing but to do good and to bring about comity.’ (62)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ مَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَعِظْهُمْ وَقُل لَّهُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَوْلًۢا بَلِيغًا (٦٣)
```

```english
They are the ones whom Allah knows as to what is in their hearts. So let them alone, and advise them, and speak to them concerning themselves far-reaching words. (63)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذ ظَّلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ جَآءُوكَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابًا رَّحِيمًا (٦٤)
```

```english
We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave. Had they, when they wronged themselves, come to you and pleaded to Allah for forgiveness, and the Apostle had pleaded for them [to Allah]for forgiveness, they would have surely found Allah all-clement, all-merciful. (64)
```

```arabic
فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا (٦٥)
```

```english
But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their hearts any dissent to your verdict and submit in full submission. (65)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَنِ ٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ أَوِ ٱخْرُجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِكُم مَّا فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا۟ مَا يُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتًا (٦٦)
```

```english
Had We prescribed for them, [commanding]: ‘Slay [the guilty among] your folks or leave your habitations,’ they would not have done it except a few of them. And if they had done as they were advised, it would have been better for them and stronger in confirming [their faith]. (66)
```

```arabic
وَإِذًا لَّـَٔاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّن لَّدُنَّآ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٦٧)
```

```english
Then We would surely have given them from Us a great reward, (67)
```

```arabic
وَلَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (٦٨)
```

```english
and We would have guided them to a straight path. (68)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَٱلصِّدِّيقِينَ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءِ وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ ۚ وَحَسُنَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ رَفِيقًا (٦٩)
```

```english
Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they! (69)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَضْلُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ عَلِيمًا (٧٠)
```

```english
That is the grace of Allah, and Allah suffices as knower [of His creatures]. (70)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses—as you can see—are not lacking in connection to those that preceded them. Indeed, the verses of this Surah, starting from His saying, the Exalted: **وَاعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَلا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً** (Worship Allah and do not ascribe any partner to Him [4:36]), seem to be directed toward encouraging people to spend in the way of Allah (الإنفاق في سبيل الله), strengthening the backbone of the social classes and the needy among the believers, and censuring those who hinder people from undertaking this obligatory project. This is followed by the exhortation to obey Allah, the Messenger, and the Vestiges of Authority (أولي الأمر), uprooting the sources of disagreement, avoiding quarreling and contention, and referring such matters back to Allah and His Messenger should they occur. It further warns against hypocrisy (النفاق) and emphasizes the necessity of submission (التسليم) to the commands of Allah and His Messenger, and so on, until it reaches the verses summoning to struggle (الجهاد), explaining its rulings, or commanding the mobilization in the way of Allah. Thus, all these verses equip the believers for struggle in the way of Allah and organize their internal affairs. Occasionally, a verse or two may intervene as a parenthetical statement (الاعتراض) in the discourse, which does not disrupt the continuity of the speech, as previously alluded to regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكارى** (O you who have faith! Do not approach prayer when you are intoxicated [4:43]).

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** (O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you [4:59]). When He finished the exhortation to worship Allah alone without any partner, the spreading of benevolence among the classes of believers, and the censure of those who find fault with this praiseworthy path or turn away from it, He returned to the primary objective in a different manner. From this objective branch out other secondary matters by which the foundation of Islamic society is fortified—namely, the incitement and encouragement toward harmony (الائتلاف) and agreement (الاتفاق), and the removal of every occurring contention by referring it back to Allah and His Messenger.

There should be no doubt that His saying: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ** (Obey Allah and obey the Apostle) is a sentence brought forth as a preamble (تمهيد) and a preparation (توطئة) for the command to refer the matter back to Allah and His Messenger upon the appearance of contention, even if the content of the sentence is the foundation of all divine laws (الشرائع) and rulings (الأحكام).

This is evident from the branching (تفريع) of His saying: **فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ** (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle [4:59]).

Then there is the repeated return to this meaning in His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يَزْعُمُونَ** (Have you not regarded those who claim... [4:60]), and His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** (We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave [4:64]), and His saying: **فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ** (But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes [4:65]).

There should be no doubt that Allah, the Glorified, does not intend by obedience to Him (إطاعته) anything other than obedience to Him in what He reveals to us through His Messenger of knowledge (المعارف) and laws (الشرائع). As for His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he possesses two aspects (حيثيتان): First, the aspect of legislation (التشريع) through what his Lord reveals to him outside of the Book—which is what he explains to the people regarding the details of what the Book contains in general (إجماله) and what relates to it, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ** (We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them [16:44]). Second, what he deems correct in judgment (الرأي), which is linked to his authority (ولايته) in governance (الحكومة) and adjudication (القضاء). He, the Exalted, said: **لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ** (That you may judge between people by what Allah has shown you [4:105]). This is the judgment by which he would rule based on the outward (ظواهر) laws of adjudication among the people, and it is that by which he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would decide in decisive matters (عزائم الأمور). Allah, the Glorified, had commanded him to adopt a judgment through consultation (المشاورة), saying: **وَشاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ فَإِذا عَزَمْتَ فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ** (And consult them in the affairs; and once you are resolved, put your trust in Allah [3:159]). Thus, He associated them with him in consultation but singled him out in the final resolve (العزم).

Once you recognize this, you will know that obedience to the Messenger has one meaning and obedience to Allah, the Glorified, has another, even if obedience to the Messenger is, in reality, obedience to Allah—since Allah is the Legislator (المشرع) of the obligation of obeying him, as He said: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** (We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave). Therefore, it is upon the people to obey the Messenger in what he clarifies through revelation (الوحي) and in what he deems correct in judgment (الرأي).

This meaning—and Allah knows best—is what necessitated the repetition of the command for obedience in His saying: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ** (Obey Allah and obey the Apostle), and not what the commentators mentioned: that the repetition is for emphasis (التأكيد). For if the intent were related to emphasis, the omission of the repetition—as if it were said: "Obey Allah and the Messenger"—would have been more indicative and closer to it, as it would imply that obedience to the Messenger is identical to obedience to Allah, the Glorified, and that the two obediences are one. Not every repetition signifies emphasis.

As for the Vestiges of Authority (أولو الأمر) — whoever they may be — they have no share in revelation (الوحي); rather, their function is the judgment (الرأي) they deem correct. Thus, they have the right to the assumption of obedience (افتراض الطاعة) similar to what the Messenger has in his judgment and word. For this reason, when He mentioned the obligation of referral (الرد) and submission (التسليم) during a dispute, He did not mention them, but rather restricted it to Allah and the Messenger, saying: **فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ** (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle, if you believe in Allah and the Last Day [4:59]). This is because those addressed with this referral are the believers addressed at the beginning of the verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (O you who have faith!). The contention is undoubtedly their contention, and it is not permissible to assume their contention with the Vestiges of Authority while their obedience is mandated. Rather, this contention is what occurs among the believers themselves, and it is not regarding a matter of judgment (الرأي), but rather regarding the ruling of Allah (حكم الله) in the disputed case, as evidenced by the subsequent verses censuring those who refer to the judgment of the Taghut (الطاغوت) — *the rebellious powers that exceed the limits set by Allah* — instead of the judgment of Allah and His Messenger. In this judgment, it is obligatory to refer to the clarified and established rulings of the religion in the Book and the Sunnah (السنة) — *the prophetic tradition*. The Book and the Sunnah are two definitive proofs (حجتان قاطعتان) in the matter for whoever is capable of understanding the ruling from them. The word of the Vestiges of Authority stating that the Book and the Sunnah rule such-and-such is also a definitive proof, for the verse establishes the mandate of obedience without any condition or restriction, and everything ultimately refers back to the Book and the Sunnah.

From here it becomes clear that these Vestiges of Authority—whoever they may be—do not have the right to establish a new ruling, nor to abrogate (ينسخوا) a ruling established in the Book and the Sunnah. Otherwise, there would be no meaning to the obligation of referring cases of contention to the Book and the Sunnah and the referral to Allah and the Messenger, as indicated by His saying: **وَما كانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَلا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذا قَضَى اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ أَمْراً أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ وَمَنْ يَعْصِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلالاً مُبِيناً** (A faithful man or woman may not have any option in their matter, when Allah and His Apostle have decided on a matter, and whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle has certainly strayed into manifest error [33:36]). Allah’s decision is legislation (التشريع), and His Messenger’s decision is either that or more general. What they do have is the right to exercise their judgment in matters where their authority (الولاية) is effective, and to uncover the ruling of Allah and His Messenger in general issues and subjects.

In short, since these Vestiges of Authority have no choice (خيرة) in the laws (الشرائع), and they possess nothing except the ruling of Allah and His Messenger—namely the Book and the Sunnah—Allah, the Glorified, did not mention them a second time when mentioning the referral in His saying: **فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ** (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle). Thus, for Allah, the Exalted, there is one obedience, and for the Messenger and the Vestiges of Authority, there is one obedience; therefore He said: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** (Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you).

There should be no doubt that this obedience commanded in His saying: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ** (Obey Allah and obey the Apostle) is an absolute obedience (إطاعة مطلقة), unconditioned by any condition and unrestricted by any restriction. This is the proof that the Messenger does not command anything, nor forbid anything, that opposes the ruling of Allah in an event; otherwise, the mandate of his obedience would be a contradiction from Him, the Exalted and Sanctified. This cannot be achieved except through inerrancy (العصمة) — *the divine protection from error and sin* — in him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

This same argument applies to the Vestiges of Authority. However, since the existence of the power of inerrancy in the Messenger is established by proofs from both reason (العقل) and tradition (النقل) in itself, independent of this verse, whereas it is not apparently so for the Vestiges of Authority, someone might imagine that inerrancy is not required in them, and that the verse does not depend on it for the soundness of its meaning.

To clarify: what the verse establishes is a ruling enacted for the benefit of the community (الأمة), by which the society of Muslims is protected from the infiltration of disagreement and fragmentation and the splitting of their ranks. Thus, it does not exceed the customary authority (الولاية) known among nations and societies, which grants an individual the mandate of obedience and the effectiveness of their word. They know that he might occasionally disobey or err in his judgment; however, if his opposition to the law in his judgment is known, he is not obeyed in that, and he is alerted to where he erred. In cases where his error is merely possible, his judgment is executed even if he is mistaken in reality, and his error is not heeded. For the interest of preserving the unity of society and avoiding the fragmentation of the word is an interest that compensates for such mistakes and oversights.

This would be the state of the Vestiges of Authority mentioned in the verse regarding the mandate of their obedience: Allah mandated their obedience upon the believers. If they command what opposes the Book and the Sunnah, that is not permissible for them and their ruling is not effective, due to the saying of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: **لا طاعة لمخلوق في معصية الخالق** — "There is no obedience to a created being in disobedience to the Creator." Both groups [Sunni and Shi'a] have narrated this meaning, and by it, the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse is restricted. As for error and mistake, if it is known, it is referred back to the truth, which is the ruling of the Book and the Sunnah. If his error is only possible, his judgment is executed just as in what is known to be error-free. There is no harm in the obligation of acceptance and the mandate of obedience in what opposes reality in this type, because the interest of preserving unity in the community and maintaining its sovereignty and prestige compensates for this opposition. This returns to what is established in the principles of jurisprudence (أصول الفقه) regarding the probative force of apparent paths (حجية الطرق الظاهرية) while the real rulings (الأحكام الواقعية) remain as they are; when their indication opposes reality, the necessary corruption is compensated for by the interest of the path (مصلحة الطريق).

In short, the obedience of the Vestiges of Authority is mandated even if they are not inerrant and are susceptible to transgression (الفسق) and error. If they transgress, there is no obedience to them; if they err, they are referred back to the Book and the Sunnah if that is known of them, and their judgment is executed in what is not known. There is no harm in executing what opposes the ruling of Allah in reality (الواقع) but not in appearance (الظاهر), out of regard for the interest of Islam and the Muslims and to preserve the unity of the word.

By reflecting on the explanation we have provided, you will recognize the total collapse of this doubt. This is because we could potentially accept this approach in restricting the absolute nature of the verse in the case of transgression (الفسق) based on the aforementioned saying of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "There is no obedience to a created being in disobedience to the Creator," and the Quranic verses that convey this meaning, such as His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ** (Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency [7:28]), and other verses of this meaning.

Similarly, it is possible—indeed, it occurs—that a legal status similar to this aforementioned apparent probative force (الحجية الظاهرية) be established, such as the mandate of obedience to the commanders of expeditions (أمراء السرايا) whom the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to appoint over them, as well as the governors he appointed over cities like Mecca and Yemen, or those he left in charge of Medina when he went out for battle, and the probative force of the mujtahid’s (المجتهد) — *a jurist capable of independent legal reasoning* — word over his follower (المقلد), and so on. However, this does not necessitate the restriction of the verse. For a matter being correct in itself is one thing, and it being the intended meaning of the outward (ظاهر) text of a Quranic verse is another.

The verse indicates the mandate of obedience to these Vestiges of Authority and has not restricted it with any condition or restriction. There is nothing in the Quranic verses that restricts the verse in its meaning such that the meaning of His saying: **وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** (And obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you) would become like our saying: "And obey the Vestiges of Authority among you as long as they do not command a sin or you do not know of their error; if they command you to sin, there is no obedience upon you, and if you know their error, correct them by referral to the Book and the Sunnah." This is not the meaning of His saying: **وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ**.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, clarified what is clearer than this restriction in matters of lesser importance than this mandated obedience, such as His saying regarding parents: **وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ حُسْناً وَإِنْ جاهَداكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِي ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلا تُطِعْهما** (We have enjoined man to be good to his parents. But if they strive with you to make you ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them [29:8]). Why then did He not manifest any of these restrictions in a verse that contains the very foundation of the religion, and to which all the roots of human felicity (السعادة) trace back?

Moreover, the verse joined the Messenger and the Vestiges of Authority together and mentioned for both of them a single obedience, saying: **وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** (And obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you). It is not possible for the Messenger to command a sin or to err in a ruling. If any of that were possible for the Vestiges of Authority, it would have been necessary to mention the restriction applicable to them. Thus, there is no escape from taking the verse as absolute (مطلقة) without any restriction, and its necessary implication is the requirement of inerrancy (العصمة) on the part of the Vestiges of Authority just as it was required on the part of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, without any difference.

Furthermore, what is meant by "the affair" (الأمر) in "the Vestiges of Authority" (أولي الأمر) is the matter pertaining to the religion of the believers addressed by this discourse or their worldly life, as supported by His saying, the Exalted: **وَشاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ** (And consult them in the affairs [3:159]), and His saying in praise of the God-conscious: **وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورى بَيْنَهُمْ** (And their affairs are by counsel among themselves [42:38]), even if it is possible in some way that "the affair" (الأمر) refers to the opposite of "the prohibition" (النهي), though this is unlikely.

It is restricted by His saying: **مِنْكُمْ** (among you), and its outward meaning is that it is a stable prepositional phrase—meaning Vestiges of Authority who are from among you. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **هُوَ الَّذِي بَعَثَ فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ رَسُولاً مِنْهُمْ** (It is He who sent to the unlettered [people] an apostle from among themselves [62:2]), and His saying in the prayer of Abraham: **رَبَّنا وَابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولاً مِنْهُمْ** (Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them [2:129]), and His saying: **رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ آياتِي** (Apostles from among you, relating to you My signs [7:35]). By this, what some have mentioned is refuted: that restricting the Vestiges of Authority with the phrase "among you" (مِنْكُمْ) indicates that each one of them is an ordinary human being like us, and they are from us and we are believers without any distinction of divine inerrancy.

Furthermore, since "the Vestiges of Authority" (أولو الأمر) is a collective noun (اسم جمع) indicating a collective plurality in those named as Vestiges of Authority, there is no doubt about this. However, it is possible at first glance that they are individuals who take charge of the affair and are clothed with the mandate of obedience one after another, so the mandate of obedience is attributed to all of them according to the wording and by taking the commonality of the meaning, like our saying: "Perform your obligatory prayers and obey your masters and the elders of your people."

Among the strange statements is what al-Razi mentioned: that this meaning necessitates treating the plural as a singular, which is contrary to the outward meaning (خلاف الظاهر). He overlooked that this is a common usage in the language, and the Quran is full of it, such as His saying, the Exalted: **فَلا تُطِعِ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ** (So do not obey the deniers [68:8]), and His saying: **فَلا تُطِعِ الْكافِرِينَ** (So do not obey the faithless [25:52]), and His saying: **إِنَّا أَطَعْنا سادَتَنا وَكُبَراءَنا** (We obeyed our leaders and elders [33:67]), and His saying: **وَلا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ** (And do not obey the bid of the prodigal [26:151]), and His saying: **حافِظُوا عَلَى الصَّلَواتِ** (Be watchful of your prayers [2:238]), and His saying: **وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (And lower your wing to the believers [15:88]), among other various instances of affirmation and negation, report and command.

What is truly contrary to the outward meaning in treating the plural as singular is to utter the plural term while intending one of its individuals, not to apply a ruling to the plural such that it decomposes into multiple rulings by the multiplicity of individuals, like our saying: "Honor the scholars of your city," meaning honor this scholar, and honor that scholar, and so on.

It is also possible that what is meant by the Vestiges of Authority—those who are the object of the mandate of obedience—is the collective as a collective, i.e., the entity (الهيئة) resulting from a specific number, each of whom is one of the Vestiges of Authority, being someone with influence among the people and impact on their affairs, such as the leaders of armies and expeditions, the scholars, the guardians of the state, and the nobles of the people. Indeed, as mentioned in *al-Manar*, they are "the people of loosening and binding" (أهل الحل والعقد) — *the influential leaders and scholars who represent the community* — whom the community trusts, including scholars, military leaders, and those in charge of public interests like trade, industry, and agriculture, as well as labor leaders, party leaders, and the directors and editors of respected newspapers! This is the meaning of the Vestiges of Authority being the people of loosening and binding, and they are the social entity (الهيئة الاجتماعية) from the prominent figures of the community. However, the issue lies in applying the content of the entire verse to this possibility.

The verse indicates—as you have recognized—the inerrancy (العصمة) of the Vestiges of Authority, and those commentators who hold this view have been forced to accept that.

Is the one characterized by this inerrancy the individuals of this entity, such that every single one of them is inerrant, and thus the whole is inerrant since the collective is nothing but the individuals? But it is self-evident that not a single day has passed for this community in which a group of the people of loosening and binding, all of whom were inerrant, gathered to execute an affair of the community. It is impossible that Allah would command something that has no instance (مصداق) in external reality. Or is this inerrancy—which is a real attribute (صفة حقيقية)—subsisting in that entity as an attribute subsists in its subject, even if the parts and individuals are not inerrant, but rather polytheism (الشرك) and sin are possible for them as they are for all other individuals, such that the judgment an individual holds may be erroneous and a call to misguidance and sin, unlike what the aforementioned entity holds due to its inerrancy? This too is impossible. How can a conventional subject (موضوع اعتباري) be conceived as being characterized by a real attribute—namely, the characterization of a social entity with inerrancy?

Or is it that the inerrancy of this entity is not an attribute of its individuals nor of the entity itself, but its reality is that Allah protects this entity from commanding a sin or holding a judgment and erring in it, just as a mass-transmitted report (الخبر المتواتر) is protected from falsehood, yet this inerrancy is not an attribute of each of the reporters nor of the social entity? Rather, its reality is that custom (العادة) proceeds upon the impossibility of falsehood in it. In other words, He, the Exalted, protects the report of this nature from the occurrence of error in it and the infiltration of falsehood into it. Thus, the judgment of the Vestiges of Authority would be something in which error never occurs, even if neither their individuals nor their entity are characterized by an additional attribute; rather, it is like the mass-transmitted report, protected from falsehood and error. Let this be the meaning of inerrancy in the Vestiges of Authority. The verse does not indicate more than that their judgment is not stumbling but correct, agreeing with the Book and the Sunnah, and it is from the providence (عناية) of Allah upon the community. It has been narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said: "My community will not gather upon an error."

As for the narration, it is foreign to the subject. For if it is authentic, it only negates the gathering of the community (الأمة) upon an error, and does not negate the gathering of the people of loosening and binding (أهل الحل والعقد) from among them upon an error. "The community" has one meaning and "the people of loosening and binding" has another, and there is no proof for intending the second meaning from the first term. Likewise, it does not negate error from the gathering of the community, but negates the gathering *upon* an error, and there is a difference between the two.

The meaning of the narration returns to the fact that error in a particular matter does not encompass the entire community; rather, there will always be among them someone who is upon the truth: either all of them or some of them, even if it is one inerrant individual. This agrees with what the verses and narrations indicate: that the religion of Islam and the creed of truth will not be removed from the earth but will remain until the Day of Resurrection. He, the Exalted, said: **فَإِنْ يَكْفُرْ بِها هؤُلاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنا بِها قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ** (So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will not disbelieve in them [6:89]), and His saying: **وَجَعَلَها كَلِمَةً باقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ** (And he made it a lasting word among his posterity [43:28]), and His saying: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ** (Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it [15:9]), and His saying: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** (Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it [41:41-42]), among other verses.

This is not exclusive to the community of Muhammad; rather, authentic narrations indicate the contrary. These are the narrations reported through various paths from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله indicating that the Jews split into seventy-one sects, the Christians into seventy-two sects, and the Muslims will split into seventy-three sects, all of them perishing except one. We have cited the narration in the traditional discussion (المبحث الروائي) placed under His saying, the Exalted: **وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعاً** (And hold fast to the rope of Allah together [3:103]).

In short, there is no dispute over the text of the narration if its chain of transmission is authentic, for it is foreign to the subject of discussion. The discussion is rather about the meaning of the inerrancy of the people of loosening and binding from the community, if that were what is intended by His saying: **وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** (And those vested with authority among you).

What is the factor that necessitates the inerrancy of the people of loosening and binding from the Muslims in the judgment they hold? This group whose function is loosening and binding in affairs is not exclusive to the Muslim community; rather, every great nation—even small nations, tribes, and clans—is not devoid of a number of individuals who have a position in their society with power and influence in public affairs. If you examine history in past events and the nations and generations of our time, you will find many instances where their people of loosening and binding gathered in important and decisive matters upon a judgment they deemed correct and then followed it with action. Sometimes they were correct and sometimes they erred. While error in individual opinions is more frequent than in social opinions, social opinions are not such that they do not accept error at all. History and observation testify to very many such instances and cases.

If the social judgment of the people of loosening and binding in Islam were protected from error, it would only be by a factor that is not of the nature of ordinary factors, but a factor of the nature of miraculous (معجزة) factors that break the norm (خارقة للعادة). It would then be a brilliant miracle (كرامة) exclusive to this community, strengthening their backbone, protecting their sanctuary, and guarding them from every evil that creeps into their group and unity. Ultimately, it would be a miraculous divine cause following the Noble Quran and living as long as the Quran lives; its relation to the practical life of the community would be like the relation of the Quran to their intellectual life. It would then have been necessary for the Quran to clarify its limits and the breadth of its circle, and for Allah to bestow it as a favor just as He bestowed the Quran and Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله as favors. It would have been necessary to clarify for this group their social duty just as He clarified that for His Prophet, and for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to enjoin his community regarding it, especially his noble companions who became the people of loosening and binding after him and assumed the guardianship of the community's affairs. It would have been necessary to clarify what the reality of this group called "the Vestiges of Authority" is, what its limit is, and what the scope of its work is. Does it form a single governing body over all Muslims in public affairs for the entire Islamic community? Or does a council of Vestiges of Authority convene in every Islamic society to rule over their lives, honors, and properties?

It would have been necessary for the Muslims—especially the companions—to be concerned with it, to ask about it and investigate it. They asked about things of no value compared to this important matter, such as the new moons, what they should spend, and the spoils of war. He, the Exalted, said: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ** (They question you concerning the new moons [2:189]), and **يَسْئَلُونَكَ ما ذا يُنْفِقُونَ** (They question you as to what they should spend [2:215]), and **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ** (They question you concerning the spoils of war [8:1]). Why then did they not ask? Or did they ask and then hands played with it until it became hidden from us? The matter is not something that opposes the desire of the majority of the community proceeding on this path such that they would suppress it by turning away and abandoning it until it is forgotten.

It would have been obligatory to use it as an argument in the disagreements and tribulations (الفتن) that occurred after the departure of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله time after time. Why is it that no trace or sign of this reality is found in their arguments and debates, which the transmitters have recorded word for word and letter for letter, and it is not found in any speech or book? Nor did it appear among the early commentators from the companions and the followers (التابعين) until a small group of later scholars—al-Razi and some after him—held it!.

Even al-Razi raised an objection to this view after mentioning it: that it opposes the composite consensus (الإجماع المركب), for the opinions regarding the meaning of the Vestiges of Authority do not exceed four: the Rightly Guided Caliphs, the commanders of expeditions, the scholars, and the inerrant Imams. Thus, the fifth opinion is a breach of consensus. Then he answered that it actually refers back to the third opinion, thereby spoiling what he had attempted to rectify. All of this dictates that the matter was not of this status, and it was not understood from it that it is a noble gift and a precious endowment from the miracles of Islam and its extraordinary favors for the people of loosening and binding among the Muslims.

Or it might be said: this inerrancy does not trace back to a factor that breaks the norm; rather, Islam built its general upbringing on precise principles that produce this result: that the people of loosening and binding of the community do not err in what they gather upon, and error does not affect them in what they judge.

This possibility—besides being false due to its contradiction of the general law (الناموس العام), which is that the perception of the whole is the sum of the perceptions of the parts, and if error is possible for every single one, it is possible for the whole—is refuted by the fact that if the judgment of the Vestiges of Authority in this sense relied for its correctness and inerrancy on such an invincible factor, it would not fail to produce its effect. So where do these falsehoods and corruptions that have filled the Islamic world end?

How many Islamic assemblies after the departure of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saw the people of loosening and binding among the Muslims gather upon what they gathered upon, then follow a path to which their judgment guided them, only to increase in nothing but misguidance, and their "felicity" for the Muslims increased in nothing but wretchedness? The religious gathering after the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not last long before it returned to a tyrannical, crushing empire! Let the critical researcher investigate the tribulations that arose since the passing of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and what they entailed of shed blood, violated honors, plundered wealth, suspended rulings, and abolished penalties! Then let him investigate their origin, their source, their roots, and their lineages—do the causes acting within them trace back to anything other than what the people of loosening and binding of the community judged and then carried upon the shoulders of the people?

This is the state of this firm pillar upon which the building of the religion relies—namely, the judgment of the people of loosening and binding—if it were what is intended by the inerrant Vestiges of Authority in their judgment.

Thus, there is no escape, on the view that the Vestiges of Authority are the people of loosening and binding, from saying that their error is possible and that they are like all other people, being correct and erring. However, since they were a virtuous group, expert in affairs, trained and experienced, their error is very minimal. The command for the obligation of their obedience, despite the fact that they might occasionally stumble and err, is of the nature of indulgence (المسامحة) in cases of error in view of the predominant interest in their involvement. Thus, if they ruled with what contradicts the ruling of the Book and the Sunnah, and matches what they identified as the interest of the community—by interpreting a ruling of the religion differently than it was previously interpreted, or changing a ruling to what suits the welfare of the time, the nature of the community, or the present state of the world—it would be what is followed, and it is what the religion would approve. For it desires nothing but the felicity of society and its progress in its social life, as is the apparent view seen from the conduct of the Islamic governments in the early period of Islam and those below them. No ruling among the rulings circulating in the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was prevented, nor was any of his conducts and traditions (سننه) decided against, except that it was justified by saying that the previous ruling conflicted with one of the rights of the community, and that the welfare of the community lay in executing a new ruling that rectifies their state, or establishing a modern tradition that matches their hopes for the felicity of life. Some researchers (1) have explicitly stated that the Caliph has the right to act in contradiction to the explicit text of the religion to preserve the welfare of the community.

Accordingly, the state of the Islamic creed would be like the state of all other virtuous civil societies, in that there is an elected assembly that rules over the laws of society according to what it sees and observes of the requirements of circumstances and the necessities of situations.

This approach or view—as you can see—is the view of one who sees that religion is a social tradition (سنة اجتماعية) cast in the mold of religion and appearing in its form. Thus, it is subject to what the cores and structures of human societies are subject to in terms of evolution through stages of gradual perfection, and it is a high example that only applies to the life of the human who lived in the era of prophecy and what is near it.

It is a passing link in the links of this chain called human society, which should not be investigated today except as geologists (علماء طبقات الأرض) investigate artifacts extracted from beneath the layers of the earth.

Whoever holds such a view, we have no discussion with him regarding this verse: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** (Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you). For the view is built on a principle that affects all the principles and traditions inherited from the religion—from original knowledge (معارف أصلية), ethical laws (نواميس أخلاقية), and secondary rulings (أحكام فرعية). If what occurred from the companions in the time of the Prophet and during his terminal illness, then the disagreements that issued from them, and what occurred of the Caliphs' disposal of some rulings and some of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله conduct, then in the time of Mu'awiyah and those who followed him from the Umayyads, then the Abbasids, then those who followed them—all being similar matters—were interpreted according to this, it would produce a staggering result.

Among the most strange statements related to this verse is what some authors mentioned: that the saying of the Exalted: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** does not indicate any of what the commentators mentioned despite their differing views.

First, because the mandate of obedience to the Vestiges of Authority—whoever they may be—does not indicate any virtue or distinction for them over others at all, just as the obedience to tyrants and oppressors is obligatory upon us in a state of necessity (الاضطرار) to guard against their evil, and they will never be better than us before Allah, the Glorified, because of that.

Second, because the ruling mentioned in the verse does not exceed other rulings whose actuality (فعلية) depends on the realization of their subjects (موضوعاتها), similar to the obligation of spending on the poor and the prohibition of aiding the oppressor; it is not obligatory for us to create a poor person so that we may spend on him, or an oppressor so that we may not aid him.

The two points he mentioned are clearly false, in addition to the fact that this speaker assumed that what is meant by the Vestiges of Authority in the verse are the rulers and sultans, and the falsity of this possibility has been clarified.

As for the first point, he overlooked that the Quran is full of prohibitions against obeying the oppressors, the prodigal, and the faithless. It is impossible that Allah would, despite that, command their obedience and then go further to join their obedience with the obedience of Himself and His Messenger. If this obedience were assumed to be the obedience of dissimulation (التقية), it would have been expressed with "permission" and the like, as He, the Exalted, said: **إِلَّا أَنْ تَتَّقُوا مِنْهُمْ تُقاةً** (Unless you guard yourselves against them [3:28]), not by explicitly commanding their obedience such that it entails every hideous consequence.

As for the second point, it is built on the first point regarding the meaning of the verse. However, if the mandate of their obedience were assumed because of their status in the religion, they would be inerrant for the reasons detailed previously. It is impossible that Allah would command the obedience of someone who has no instance (مصداق), or has an accidental instance, in a verse that contains the very foundation of religious interests and a ruling without which the state of Islamic society cannot be upright at all. You have recognized that the need for the Vestiges of Authority is identical to the need for the Messenger, which is the need for the guardianship (ولاية) of the community's affair, and we have discussed this in the discussion on the clear (المحكم) and the ambiguous (المتشابه).

Let us return to the beginning of the discussion on the verse:

It has become clear to you from all that we have presented that there is no meaning in interpreting His saying, the Exalted: **وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** (And those vested with authority among you) as the group of those who gather from the people of loosening and binding, which is the social entity in whatever sense we interpret it. Thus, it is only that what is meant by the Vestiges of Authority are individuals from the community who are inerrant in their words and whose obedience is mandated. Therefore, knowing them requires explicit designation (تنصيص) from Allah, the Glorified, in His speech or through the tongue of His Prophet. This applies to what has been narrated through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) that they are the ones.

As for what has been said—that the Vestiges of Authority are the Rightly Guided Caliphs, or the commanders of expeditions, or the scholars followed in their words and judgments—all of that is refuted: first, because the verse indicates their inerrancy, and there is no inerrancy in these classes without doubt, except for what a group of Muslims believes regarding Ali (peace be upon him); and second, because each of the three views is a statement without proof to support it.

As for the objections raised against it being the inerrant Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them):

First: That this requires an explicit definition from Allah and His Messenger, and if that were the case, no two people would have disagreed about them after the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The response: That is explicitly stated in the Book and the Sunnah, such as the Verse of Guardianship (آية الولاية) and the Verse of Purification (آية التطهير) and others, and the detailed discussion of them will follow. Also, the Hadith of the Ark: "The likeness of my Ahl al-Bayt is like the Ark of Noah; whoever boards it is saved, and whoever stays behind is drowned," and the Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (حديث الثقلين): "I am leaving among you the two weighty things: the Book of Allah and my progeny, the people of my house—as long as you hold fast to them both, you will never go astray after me." This was mentioned in the discussion of the clear and the ambiguous in the third volume of the book. Also, the hadiths of the Vestiges of Authority narrated through the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis, some of which will appear in the following traditional discussion.

Second: That their obedience is conditioned by knowing them (معرفتهم), for without knowing them, it is a task beyond one's capacity (تكليف بما لا يطاق). If it is conditioned, the verse refutes it because it is absolute.

The response: The objection is turned back upon the objector, for obedience is conditioned by knowledge (المعرفة) absolutely. The only difference is that for the people of loosening and binding, their instance is known by ourselves without need for clarification from Allah and His Messenger, while the inerrant Imam requires a definer to identify him. There is no difference between one condition and another in its contradiction to the verse.

Furthermore, although knowledge is counted as a condition, it is not like other conditions. It pertains to the realization of the reaching of the obligation (بلوغ التكليف); thus, there is no obligation without knowledge of it, its subject, and its object. It does not pertain to the obligation (التكليف) and the obligated act (المكلف به) themselves. If knowledge were in the same category as other conditions like financial ability (الاستطاعة) for Hajj and finding water for ablution (الوضوء), for example, no absolute obligation would ever exist, since there is no meaning to directing an obligation toward an obligated person whether he knows of it or not.

Third: That in our time, we are unable to reach the inerrant Imam and learn knowledge and religion from him; thus, he cannot be the one whose obedience Allah mandated upon the community, since there is no way to him.

The response: That is attributed to the community itself in its evil deeds and its betrayal of itself, not to Allah and His Messenger. Thus, the obligation is not lifted, just as if the community killed its Prophet and then excused itself that it is unable to obey him. Moreover, the objection is turned back upon him, for today we are unable to find a single nation in Islam in which what its people of loosening and binding deem correct is executed.

Fourth: That Allah, the Exalted, says: **فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ** (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle). If what was meant by the Vestiges of Authority were the inerrant Imam, it would have been obligatory to say: "Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it to the Imam."

The response: Its answer was provided in the preceding explanation; what is meant by the referral is the referral to the Imam by the approach previously mentioned.

Fifth: That those who hold the view of the inerrant Imam say: the benefit of following him is to save the community from the darkness of disagreement and the harm of contention and division. The outward meaning of the verse explains the ruling of contention despite the existence of the Vestiges of Authority and the community's obedience to them, as if the Vestiges of Authority were to disagree regarding the ruling of some novel events and occurrences. Disagreement and contention in the presence of the inerrant Imam is not permissible according to those who hold this view, because he is like the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to them; thus, this addition would have no benefit according to their view.

The response: Its answer is also clear from what preceded. The contention mentioned in the verse is only the contention of the believers regarding the rulings of the Book and the Sunnah, not the rulings of guardianship (أحكام الولاية) issued by the Imam in events and incidents. It has been previously stated that there is no ruling except for Allah and His Messenger. If the contenders are able to understand the ruling from the Book and the Sunnah, they may derive it from them, or ask the Imam about it, and he is inerrant in his understanding. If they are unable to do that, they must ask the Imam about it. This is similar to those who were contemporaries of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: they would gain deep understanding (يتفقهون) in what they were able to, or ask the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about it, and they would ask him regarding what they were unable to understand through derivation (الاستنباط).

Thus, the ruling of the Vestiges of Authority regarding obedience is the ruling of the Messenger as indicated by the verse. The ruling of contention is what He mentioned in the verse, whether in the presence of the Messenger—as indicated by the subsequent verses—or in his absence, as indicated by the command in the verse in its absolute nature. Thus, the referral to Allah and the Messenger mentioned in the verse is exclusive to the case of the believers' contention, as indicated by His saying: **تَنازَعْتُمْ** (you fall out), and He did not say: "If the Vestiges of Authority fall out," nor did He say: "If they fall out." The referral to Allah and the Messenger during the presence of the Messenger is to ask the Messenger about the ruling of the matter or its derivation from the Book and the Sunnah for those capable of it. During his absence, it is to ask the Imam about it or derivation, as previously explained. Thus, His saying: **فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ** (Then if you fall out regarding anything) etc., is not a redundant part of the speech that could be dispensed with, as the objector claimed.

It has been clarified from all that preceded: that what is meant by the Vestiges of Authority in the verse are men from the community whose ruling in inerrancy and the mandate of obedience is the ruling of the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. This, nonetheless, does not contradict the generality of the concept (مفهوم) of the term "Vestiges of Authority" according to the language, and its being intended by the term. For intending a concept from among concepts by a term is one thing, and intending the instance (المصداق) to which the concept applies is another. This is just as the concept of "Messenger" is a general, universal meaning, and it is what is intended by the term in the verse, but the intended instance is the Messenger Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ** (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle) to the end of the verse, is a branching (تفريع) from the restriction (الحصر) inferred from the context. For His saying: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ** (Obey Allah) etc., since it made the obedience of Allah and His Messenger obligatory—and this obedience is only regarding religious materials that undertake the removal of every assumed disagreement and every possible need—there remained no case where there is a need to refer to other than Allah and His Messenger. The meaning of the speech was: "Obey Allah, and do not obey the Taghut," which is the restriction we mentioned.

Directing the discourse to the believers reveals that what is meant by contention is their contention among themselves, not an assumed contention between them and the Vestiges of Authority, nor an assumed contention among the Vestiges of Authority. For the first—namely, contention between them and the Vestiges of Authority—does not suit the mandate of the Vestiges of Authority's obedience upon them. Likewise, the second—namely, contention among the Vestiges of Authority—for the mandate of obedience does not suit a contention in which one of the two sides is upon falsehood. Furthermore, it does not suit the discourse being directed to the believers in His saying: **فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ** (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it).

The word "thing" (شيء), although it encompasses every ruling and command from Allah, the Messenger, and the Vestiges of Authority, whatever it may be, His saying after that: **فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ** (refer it to Allah and the Apostle) indicates that what is assumed is the dispute regarding something in which the Vestiges of Authority do not have independence and absolute discretion (الاستبداد) from their commands within the circle of their guardianship—such as their command to mobilize, or for war, or peace, or otherwise. For there is no meaning to the obligation of referral to Allah and the Messenger in these cases while their obedience in them is mandated.

Thus, the verse indicates the obligation of referral in the religious rulings themselves, in which no one has the right to rule by execution or abrogation except Allah and His Messenger. The verse is like an explicit statement that no one has the right to dispose of a religious ruling legislated by Allah and His Messenger; the Vestiges of Authority and those below them are equal in that.

His saying: **إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ** (if you believe in Allah) is an intensification (تشديد) of the ruling and an indication that opposing it only arises from corruption in the stage of faith. Thus, the ruling is linked to it with a connection; opposition reveals a pretense of the attribute of faith in Allah and His Messenger while harboring disbelief, which is hypocrisy (النفاق), as indicated by the subsequent verses.

His saying: **ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلاً** (That is better and more favorable in outcome [4:59]), meaning the referral during contention, or the obedience to Allah, the Messenger, and the Vestiges of Authority. The outcome (التأويل) — *the ultimate reality or consequence* — is the real interest (المصلحة الواقعية) from which the ruling arises and which then results from the action. The discussion of its meaning preceded under His saying, the Exalted: **وَابْتِغاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ وَما يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللهُ** (and seeking its interpretation; and no one knows its interpretation except Allah [3:7]) in the third volume of the book.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يَزْعُمُونَ أَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ** (Have you not regarded those who claim that they believe in what has been sent down to you [4:60]) to the end of the verse. **Claim** (الزعم) is the belief in something whether it matches reality or not, unlike **knowledge** (العلم), which is the belief that matches reality. Because "claim" is used for belief in cases that do not match reality, it is sometimes thought that non-matching with reality is part of its concept, but it is not so. **Taghut** (الطاغوت) is a verbal noun meaning transgression (الطغيان), like *rahabut*, *jabarut*, and *malakut*, except that it is sometimes uttered to mean the active participle (اسم الفاعل) for emphasis. It is said: "the water transgressed" (طغى الماء) when it exceeded its container due to its abundance. Its use for a human was initially by way of metaphor (استعارة), then it became common and joined the literal meaning; it is the human's departure from the state defined for him by reason or law. Thus, the **Taghut** is the oppressor, the tyrant, and the one who rebels against the duties of servitude to Allah out of arrogance toward Him, and so on. To this returns what was said: that the Taghut is every deity worshipped besides Allah.

His saying: **بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَما أُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ** (in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you) is in the position of saying: "in what Allah sent down to His messengers." He did not say: "they believed in you and in those before you," because the discussion is about the obligation of referral to the Book of Allah and His ruling. By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by His saying: **وَقَدْ أُمِرُوا أَنْ يَكْفُرُوا بِهِ** (while they have been commanded to defy it) is the command in the heavenly Books and the revelation (الوحي) sent down to the prophets: Muhammad and those before him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ** (Have you not regarded) etc. The speech is in the position of repelling an objection (دفع الدخل), as if it were said: "What is the reason for mentioning His saying: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ** etc.?" So it was said: "Have you not regarded their failure in obedience when they desire to seek judgment from the Taghut?" The interrogation is for regret (التأسف), and the meaning is: "It is a matter of regret what you have seen—that some people, while believing that they are believers in what was sent down to you of the Book and to the rest of the prophets, and the heavenly Books were only sent down to judge between people in what they differed about—as Allah clarified to them by His saying: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** (Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed [2:213])—they seek judgment during contention from the Taghut, who are the people of transgression and those who rebel against the religion of Allah, exceeding the truth. They have been commanded in these Books to disbelieve in the Taghut, and it is sufficient in prohibiting seeking judgment from them that it is an annulment of the Books of Allah and an invalidation of His laws."

In His saying: **وَيُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُمْ ضَلالاً بَعِيداً** (and Satan desires to lead them astray into far-reaching error [4:60]), there is an indication that their seeking judgment is only by the casting of Satan and his seduction, and the direction in it is far-reaching error.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا** "And when it is said to them, ‘Come...’" (4:61) until the end of the verse. The word **تعالوا** (Come) is originally a command derived from *al-ta‘ali* (التعالي), which means rising or ascending. The phrase **يصد** (he turns away) comes from *sadda* (صد), meaning he averted or turned aside. His saying: **إِلى ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ وَإِلَى الرَّسُولِ** "to what Allah has sent down and to the Apostle" is in the position of saying: "to the judgment of Allah and the one who judges by it." In His saying: **يَصُدُّونَ عَنْكَ** "turning away from you," the Apostle was specifically mentioned as the object of their aversion—even though they were called to both the Book and the Apostle together—because the sorrow arises from the actions of those who claim to have believed in what Allah sent down. Thus, they are not disbelievers (كافرين) who would openly declare their aversion to the Book of Allah; rather, they are hypocrites (منافقون) in reality, who outwardly display faith in what Allah has sent down but turn away from His Apostle.

From here it becomes clear that making a distinction between Allah and His Apostle—by accepting the judgment of Allah while hesitating regarding the judgment of the Apostle—is absolute hypocrisy (نفاق).

His saying, the Exalted: **فَكَيْفَ إِذا أَصابَتْهُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ** "But how will it be when a misfortune strikes them..." (4:62) etc., is an announcement that this aversion and turning away from the judgment of Allah and His Apostle, and the turning toward another—namely the judgment of the *Taghut* (الطاغوت) — *the transgressive power that exceeds the bounds of Allah's law*—will result in a misfortune that strikes them, for which there is no cause except this turning away from the judgment of Allah and His Apostle and seeking judgment from the *Taghut*. His saying: **ثُمَّ جاؤُكَ يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ** "then they come to you, swearing by Allah..." is a narration of their excuse, claiming they did not intend any evil by their reliance on the judgment of the *Taghut*. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: if this is their state, how will they act when the evil consequence of their deeds strikes them, and they then come to you swearing by Allah, saying: "We intended nothing by seeking judgment from other than the Book and the Apostle except goodness, reconciliation, and the settlement of disputes between the litigants."

His saying, the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُ اللهُ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ** "They are the ones whom Allah knows what is in their hearts..." (4:63) etc., is a refutation of what they offered as an excuse. He did not explicitly mention the state of what is in their hearts—namely, a corrupt inner state—because His saying: **فأعرض عنهم وعظهم** "so turn away from them and advise them" indicates this. For if what was in their hearts were not corrupt, their words would have been truthful and right, and one is not commanded to turn away from one who speaks the truth and is sincere in his speech.

His saying: **وَقُلْ لَهُمْ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ قَوْلاً بَلِيغاً** "and speak to them reaching their souls with a far-reaching word" means a word that reaches into their souls, conveying what You want them to realize and understand regarding the corruption of this conduct, and that it is hypocrisy which, if exposed, would bring upon them woe from the wrath of Allah the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave" (4:64) is an absolute refutation of everything previously narrated regarding these hypocrites—their seeking judgment from the *Taghut*, their turning away from the Apostle, and their swearing and excusing themselves by claiming [intentions of] goodness and reconciliation. All of this constitutes opposition to the Apostle in some way, whether accompanied by an excuse or not. Allah has made obedience to him obligatory without any restriction or condition, for He did not send him except to be obeyed by Allah's leave. No one should imagine that the obedience to be followed is only the obedience to Allah, and that the Apostle is merely a human being from among His creation who is only obeyed to achieve some benefit—such that if benefit is secured without obeying him, there is no harm in acting independently to secure it while leaving the Apostle aside. [To think otherwise] would be considered associating partners with Allah (إشراكا بالله) and worshipping His Apostle alongside Him. This [misconception] would sometimes manifest in matters they discussed with the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; when he would resolve upon an important matter, one of them would say to him: "Is this an order from Allah or from you?"

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, mentioned that the obligation of obeying the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is an absolute obligation, and it is nothing other than the obedience to Allah, for it is by His leave. This is similar to what is conveyed by His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ** "Whoever obeys the Apostle certainly obeys Allah" (4:80).

Then He mentioned that if they had returned to Allah and the Apostle in repentance when they opposed the Apostle by turning away, it would have been better for them than swearing by Allah and fabricating unjustifiable excuses that neither benefit nor please the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, because Allah, the Glorified, informs him of the reality of the matter. This is His saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ جاؤُكَ** "Had they, when they wronged themselves, come to you..." (4:64) until the end of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ** "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge..." (4:65) etc. The word **الشجر** (with a *sukun* on the *jim*) and **الشجور** mean mixing or entanglement. It is said: *shajara shajran wa shujuran* (شجر شجرا وشجورا), meaning it became entangled. From this comes *al-tashajur* (التشاجر) and *al-mushajarah* (المشاجرة) — *dispute or contention* — as if the claims or words have become entangled with one another. From this, trees are called *shajar* (شجر) due to the entanglement of their branches with one another. The word **الحرج** (haraj) means constriction or tightness.

The apparent context at first glance suggests that this is a refutation of the hypocrites' claim that they believed in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while seeking judgment from the *Taghut*. Thus, the meaning is: it is not as they claim—that they believe while seeking judgment from the *Taghut*—rather, they do not believe until they make you a judge, etc.

However, the fact that the ruling of the ultimate goal—namely His saying: **حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ** "until they make you a judge"—includes those other than the hypocrites, as does His subsequent saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ** "And had We ordained for them..." until His saying: **ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ** "they would not have done it except a few of them," supports the view that the refutation is not exclusive to the hypocrites. Rather, it encompasses them and others, based on the fact that their apparent state is that they imagine that mere assent to what was sent down from Allah—including its knowledge and laws—is true faith (إيمان) in Allah, His Apostle, and what he brought from his Lord. But it is not so; rather, faith is complete submission (تسليم), both inwardly and outwardly. How can it be possible for a true believer not to submit to the Apostle's judgment outwardly by turning away from him and opposing him, or in his inner self by feeling constriction regarding the Apostle's judgment if it opposes his own desires? Allah the Exalted has said to His Apostle: **لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ** "so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you" (4:105).

Therefore, if anyone feels constriction regarding what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله has decreed, he has felt constriction regarding the judgment of Allah, for He is the One who honored him by making obedience to him mandatory and his judgment binding.

If they submit to the judgment of the Apostle and their hearts feel no constriction from it, they have certainly submitted to the judgment of Allah, whether in His legislative (التشريعي) or creative (التكويني) decree. This is one of the stations of faith (الإيمان) in which the believer is clothed with several virtuous attributes, the most prominent of which is submission (التسليم) to the command of Allah. In this station, constriction, objection, and rejection fall away from the believer's tongue and heart. In the verse, submission (التسليم) is mentioned in an absolute sense.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: **فَلا وَرَبِّكَ** "But no, by your Lord!" until the end of the verse, even if it is restricted to submission to the judgment of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله according to the literal wording—since the occasion of the verses is their seeking judgment from other than the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله despite the obligation to return to him—the meaning is general to the judgment of both Allah and His Apostle, and to both legislative and creative decrees, as you have seen.

In fact, the meaning encompasses judgment in the sense of the decree of the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and every path he took or action he performed, because the effect is shared. Everything that is attributed in any way to Allah and His Apostle, in whatever manner, cannot be rejected, objected to, tired of, or disliked in any way by a believer who truly believes in Allah. All of that constitutes polytheism (شرك) in its various degrees. Allah the Exalted has said: **وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ** "And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him" (12:106).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ** "And had We ordained for them..." until His saying: **ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ** "they would not have done it except a few of them" (4:66). It has been previously mentioned regarding His saying: **وَلكِنْ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً** "but Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness, so they do not believe except a few" (4:46), that this construction indicates that the judgment applies to the collective body of individuals—that is, the society—and that the exception is to prevent the illusion that the judgment encompasses and exhausts every single individual. Therefore, this exception is more akin to the "disjunctive exception" (المنفصل) than the "continuous exception" (المتصل), or it is an intermediate realm (برزخ) between the two types of exception because it possesses two aspects.

Accordingly, His saying: **ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ** "they would not have done it except a few of them" is presented as a report about the state of the collective whole: that they do not fulfill the restrictive and difficult laws and obligations that touch upon what their hearts are deeply attached to, such as their lives and their homes. The exception of the "few" is to dispel any misconception.

The meaning is: "And had We ordained (كتبنا)—that is, made mandatory—upon them to kill themselves or to leave their homes and familiar homelands, they would not have done it"—meaning they would not have fulfilled Our command. Then, since His saying "they would not have done it" might give the impression that there were none among them who were true believers truly submitting to Allah's judgment, He dispelled that by excepting a few of them. The judgment did not truly encompass them because the report was about the state of the society insofar as it is a society, and individuals were only included as part of the whole.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant is the killing of the collective by the collective, and the departure and exile of the collective from their collective homes, such as a town or village, rather than every single person killing himself and leaving his own house, as in His saying, the Exalted: **فَتُوبُوا إِلى بارِئِكُمْ فَاقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** "so turn to your Maker in repentance, and slay yourselves" (2:54), for the intended recipient of the address is the group, not the individuals.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا ما يُوعَظُونَ بِهِ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتاً** "And had they done what they were advised, it would have been better for them and more strengthening [for their faith]" (4:66). The replacement of "ordaining" (الكتابة) in His saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ** "And had We ordained for them" with "advice" (الوعظ) in His saying: **ما يُوعَظُونَ بِهِ** "what they were advised" is an indication that these laws, which appear in the form of commands and obligations, are nothing but pointers to what contains their welfare and happiness. Thus, they are in reality exhortations and counsels intended for their own good and well-being.

His saying: **لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ** "it would have been better for them" means in everything concerning them, in both their worldly life and the Hereafter. This is because the good of the Hereafter is inseparable from the good of this world; rather, it follows from it. His saying: **وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتاً** "and more strengthening" means for their souls and hearts in faith, since the discourse is about it. Allah the Exalted said: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ** "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word..." (14:27).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذاً لَآتَيْناهُمْ مِنْ لَدُنَّا أَجْراً عَظِيماً** "Then We would have surely given them from Us a great reward" (4:67), meaning when they become firm in steadfast faith. The discussion regarding the ambiguity of His saying: **أَجْراً عَظِيماً** "a great reward" is the same as the discussion regarding the absolute nature of His saying: **لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ** "it would have been better for them."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَهَدَيْناهُمْ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً** "and We would have surely guided them to a straight path" (4:68). The discussion regarding the meaning of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — has already passed under the commentary of His saying: **اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** "Guide us on the straight path" (1:6) in the first volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ** "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle..." until His saying: **حَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً** "and what excellent companions they are!" (4:69). He combined Allah and the Apostle in this beautiful promise, even though the preceding verses dealt with obeying the Apostle and submitting to his judgment and decree, because the mention of Allah the Exalted was interspersed among them in His saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ** "And had We ordained for them," etc. Thus, the mandated obedience is the obedience to Him, the Exalted, and the obedience to His Apostle. The discourse began in this manner in His saying: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ** "Obey Allah and obey the Apostle..." (4:59).

His saying: **فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ** "they are with those whom Allah has blessed" indicates joining (اللحوق) rather than becoming (الصيرورة). Thus, these people are joined to the group of those who have been blessed, who are the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), which in His speech, the Exalted, is not attributed to anyone other than this group in His saying: **اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ** "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed" (1:6-7). In short, they are joined to them without becoming [identical to] them, as His saying: **وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً** "and what excellent companions they are!" is not without an allusion to this. It has been previously mentioned that what is meant by this blessing (النعمة) is the guardianship (الولاية) — *the divine authority and proximity to Allah*.

As for these four groups—namely the prophets (النبيين), the truthful (الصديقين), the witnesses (الشهداء), and the righteous (الصالحين)—the prophets are the recipients of revelation (الوحي) who possess news of the Unseen (الغيب). We have no knowledge of their state beyond that except through its effects. It has been previously mentioned that what is meant by the witnesses (الشهداء) are the witnesses of deeds (شهداء الأعمال) as the term "witness" is used in the Qur'an, rather than those martyred on the battlefield. The righteous (الصالحين) are those who are worthy of Allah's blessings.

As for the truthful (الصديقون), what its wording indicates is that it is an intensive form of truthfulness (الصدق). Truthfulness can be in speech and it can be in action. Truthfulness in action is its conformity to speech, because action narrates the belief; if it is truthful in its narration, it narrates what is in the inner self without discrepancy. Truthfulness in speech is its conformity to reality. Since speech itself is a type of action, the one who is truthful in his actions only reports what he knows to be true and right. Thus, in his words, there is both objective truthfulness (الصدق الخبري) and subjective sincerity (المخبري).

So the truthful one (الصديق) who never lies at all is he who only does what he sees as the truth without following personal desire, and only says what he sees as the truth, and sees nothing but the truth. Thus, he witnesses the realities of things, speaks the truth, and acts upon the truth.
